All reports
REDX

Marketing & Messaging Guide

The guide for everyone who writes, says, or ships a word about the company.

Prepared by [Author - to be filled]
June 2026

0. How to Use This Guide

This guide turns REDX’s source of truth into positioning and copy. Every sample line below is finished copy in one committed voice: lift it, adapt it, ship it. Where the guide states the position, it states it word for word from the source of truth so every surface marches to the same line. The source of truth is the single statement three inputs resolve into, weighted to what leadership believes.

  • Internal View - what leadership believes, weighted most heavily.
  • Customer-Facing View - what the website and public materials say today.
  • Market Perception - how customers and the market see REDX now.

What each team pulls from here

  • Marketing: headlines and ad copy, Sections 5, 9.1, 9.2.
  • Sales: talk-tracks and objection responses, Sections 7.2, 9.3, 9.5.
  • Customer success: lifecycle email language, Section 9.6.
  • Partners and team leaders: the channel message and the team story, Sections 3.4 and 2.

1. The Unified Source of Truth

Everything in this guide builds up from one statement. Read it first; build every headline, deck, email, and answer downward from it.

Source of truth. REDX is the complete prospecting system, motivated-seller data, dialer, ads, mail, and content, wrapped in the category’s deepest guided coaching, that turns a willing agent’s effort into listings. The data is the on-ramp, and its accuracy is an operational reality to fix before it is amplified. The durable promise is “do the work inside our system and our coaching, and you will win,” proven by named peers who did.

The market sees a set of tools you buy: data, a dialer, and training filed under support. Leadership sees the whole, an integrated system plus the coaching that makes an agent’s work pay off, with a wall of named agents who already won inside it. The job of this guide is to build the brand perception leadership already lives in, moving the market from “REDX sells leads” to “REDX is the system that turns your work into listings.”

1.1 Brand Line Options

A CMO can write from either of these; both carry the whole.

  • “The complete prospecting system, and the coaching that turns your work into listings.”
  • “Do the work. We will hand you the system, the coaching, and the proof it pays off.”

1.2 The Three Things This Guide Holds in One Line

  • Who it is built for: the work-willing agent, the go-getter who will pick up the phone, send the mail, and run the ads, and wants a guide walking alongside them.
  • How it wins: the widest integrated channel system in the category, data plus dialer plus ads plus mail plus content, wrapped in the deepest guided coaching, proven by named agents.
  • Why it exists: to give a willing agent a roadmap to more listings, so the effort they are already prepared to spend actually pays off.

2. Messaging Pillars

Six pillars carry the single source of truth into the market, each a layer of the same platform seen from a different seat. Each lands hardest on the agents it was built for, concentrating proof where it converts.

Pillar 1: One system that does the whole job.

  1. Connects to HOW it wins: REDX is the only place an agent gets motivated-seller data, an outbound power dialer, ad building, direct mail, and content in one integrated platform, with team functionality to bring the whole team to the same prospects. No single rival holds the full combination: Mojo leads with a dialer and sells no coaching curriculum, Landvoice runs no outbound dialer, Vulcan7 and Espresso center on a single-line phone dialer with no native multi-channel orchestration. Anchor the proof on Scott Catton, who reached $223K GCI using Ad Builder, and Cindy Strait, a new agent who listed 47 homes in a year using mail merge.
  2. The move (make the market see it): lead the homepage and product pages with the integrated system, not a feature checklist. Show the channels working together: the data feeds the dialer, the dialer feeds the follow-up, the mail and ads bring sellers in. Name the integrated dialer and the team functionality as the things rivals make agents bolt together from separate vendors.
  3. What each team gets: marketing gets the “one system, every channel” hero and the integration diagram. Sales gets the “stop paying five vendors” comparison. Content and CS get the workflow story that shows the channels feeding each other.

Pillar 2: The coaching that makes your work pay off.

  1. Connects to the MISSION: REDX wraps the system in the deepest guided coaching in the category, Bootcamp, REDX University, live role-play, virtual trainings, and a community of agents doing the work. Leadership’s identity is the seasoned guide who walks alongside the agent, “we train the cowboys, we teach them how to hunt.” The proof is named: Len Nevin, a part-time agent, quit his job and now makes $250,000 prospecting expired leads; Edward Estrada, a new agent, got 5 listings in 2 days. Vulcan7 and Espresso coach too, so the differentiator is the coaching paired with the widest channel system and the deepest named-outcome library, not coaching alone.
  2. The move (make the market see it): move education out of the “Resources / Support” navigation and into the headline identity. Frame REDX as the guide, the agent as the one who does the work. Lead with the relationship, “you are not alone in the desert,” and back it with the agents the coaching produced.
  3. What each team gets: marketing gets the “you are guided the whole way” campaign line. Sales gets the role-play line and the coaching cadence as a reason to buy. CS gets the onboarding language that frames coaching as the path to the first listing.

Pillar 3: Proven by named agents, not adjectives.

  1. Connects to WHO it is built for: REDX publishes a full wall of named agents with real outcomes, the rarest asset in the category. Kayla Dieppa grew to $14M in volume in a year on expired and FSBO leads. Emily Peckham sold 160+ FSBOs in five years. Paula Burlison earns $522,000 listing homes with the Power Dialer. No rival shows this depth of named proof. This pillar replaces the worn “motivated seller” label, which Vulcan7, Espresso, and Mojo all use in the same words, with documented conversion when the lead is worked.
  2. The move (make the market see it): anchor every claim on a named agent, never an adjective. Build a structured, searchable proof library tied to product and agent type so a prospect sees the agent who looks like them. Pair the proof with the one defensible category stat: portal and internet leads convert at 0.4-1.2 percent, while REDX agents who work the lead close at rates the case studies show.
  3. What each team gets: marketing gets the proof library as the central reason to buy. Sales gets a named agent for every objection. Content gets an inexhaustible supply of real stories to publish.

Pillar 4: One ladder, an affordable on-ramp into a premium system.

  1. Connects to the commercial posture: REDX prices as one deliberate ladder. A price-sensitive new agent starts low on a single product and proves it works; the ROI buyer climbs to the full system that pays for itself. Landvoice runs $87 to $197 a month with a $40 FSBO entry; Vulcan7’s full bundle runs about $359 a month, the most expensive in its class; Espresso’s Pro plan is $279 a month. REDX sits at the low end on entry and climbs to a premium full system, two rungs of the same ladder.
  2. The move (make the market see it): sell both rungs on purpose, with no discount apology. To the new agent: start at the entry point, prove it on one product. To the ROI buyer: the full system pays for itself, and named agents show the return. Rewrite pricing copy to present the entry point and the full system as one ladder.
  3. What each team gets: marketing gets the “start small, scale up” pricing narrative. Sales gets the upgrade path scripted. CS gets the expansion conversation framed as the agent’s own growth.

Pillar 5: Honest data you can hold us to.

  1. Connects to the service relationship: REDX’s own customers report bad, disconnected, and do-not-call numbers, and data accuracy is the single most dominant negative review theme. This pillar is earn-it-then-claim-it. REDX fixes the data-hygiene experience first, de-duping do-not-call, disconnected, and already-listed numbers, tightening the confidence-rating filter, and setting honest accuracy expectations at onboarding. Then it claims parity-at-lower-cost against Vulcan7’s premium, whose edge is a “Bentley” reputation, not a measured advantage, and whose own pages cannot agree on whether it delivers “14% more” or “20% more” unique numbers.
  2. The move (make the market see it): do not headline data superiority until the experience earns it. Lead with the honest line, set accuracy expectations at onboarding, and message the confidence-rating filter as a tool the agent controls. Once the post-fix benchmark clears, claim “best-in-class data at parity with the premium competitor, at lower cost,” never a purity over-claim.
  3. What each team gets: marketing gets the parity-at-lower-cost line, gated behind the fix. Sales gets the honest-data talk-track that disarms the accuracy objection. CS gets the onboarding script that sets expectations before the first dial.

Pillar 6: Multi-channel hedges the litigation risk.

  1. Connects to HOW it wins: the most defensible reason to diversify beyond the phone is the verified surge in TCPA litigation, 2,788 cases in 2024, up 67 percent, and class actions up 95.2 percent through mid-2025, with real-estate settlements of $20M and $40M. REDX gives an agent ads, mail, and content alongside the dialer, so the business does not rest on a single channel exposed to that risk. Manual expired and FSBO calls remain legal, so the message is a prudent hedge, never “the phone is dying.”
  2. The move (make the market see it): lead the multi-channel story on the litigation-risk surge, not on a “consent rules are tightening” claim the record contradicts. Position the integrated mail, ads, and content as the channels that keep the pipeline full when an agent dials less. Keep compliance content as table-stakes support, never as the differentiator, since every vendor publishes it.
  3. What each team gets: marketing gets the “don’t bet your business on one channel” line. Sales gets the litigation-risk reframe for the compliance-anxious buyer. Content gets the multi-channel workflow as proof of the system.

Six pillars, one platform: one system that does the whole job, the coaching that makes the work pay off, proof by named agents, a price ladder anyone can enter, honest data the agent can hold us to, and a multi-channel hedge against real risk.

Note for the team on confidence. Two pillars track calls the Strategic Alignment Brief left open, so do not harden them into fixed claims yet. Pillar 6 (multi-channel hedge) rests on an open framing call: lead on the verified litigation surge, and do not run a “phone is dying” or “rules tightening” line until leadership ratifies the frame. The “beyond real estate” expansion that leadership discussed is also an open call with zero non-real-estate proof today, so no pillar above claims any vertical outside residential real estate; treat any expansion message as a funded test the company is still running. One more: Pillar 1 carries the brand-identity move (lead on system-plus-coaching and demote “best data” to an on-ramp) that the Brief tees up as a leadership decision, not a settled fact, the evidence leans this way strongly but it is a commit-with-confirmation call pending first-party retention data and leadership ratifying the identity shift, so write to it with conviction while leadership confirms it before the full public rebrand. The settled element that leads without debate is the data-fix sequence (fix the hygiene experience before amplifying any accuracy claim). Pillars 2 through 5 build on the named proof, the price ladder, and the data-fix sequence, which the Brief supports. Lead on those, and lead on Pillar 1 as the strong, confirm-before-full-commitment recommendation it is.

3. Audience

The interviews surfaced one primary buyer, the work-willing agent who needs listings, and several secondary segments, each a real seat at the same platform. The primary persona gets the full profile; the others get compact secondary blocks, each pointing back to the unified truth. A real audience never gets deleted because the strategy narrowed.

3.1 Primary Persona: The Work-Willing Prospector

  • Who they are: a real estate agent doing one or two transactions a month, generating $40,000 to $50,000 a year, watching peers pull in $200,000 to $300,000, and ready to put in the work to close that gap. “What I like most about RedX is that it is a tool that you can build your business with just that one tool alone (if you are willing to do the work).”
  • Pain: not enough listings, and no reliable system for generating and working leads. They have the willingness to prospect and need a roadmap to follow.
  • Job to be done: when they realize their pipeline is empty and the next deal is unclear, they want a system and a guide that tells them exactly what to do each day, so they can list more homes and feel in control of their business.
  • Goal: more listings, and daily clarity on what to do to get them. “More conversation equals more conversion. That’s what this platform offers and I love it.”
  • How they fit the whole: they feel every pillar, but Pillars 2 and 3 convert them, the coaching that gives them the roadmap and the named agents who prove it works. This is who the platform is built for.

3.2 Secondary Persona: The New or Part-Time Agent

  • Who they are: an ambitious new or part-time agent, “a whole lot of willingness, but not a whole lot of knowledge,” priced out of the $2,000-plus-a-month team tech stack and looking for guidance before their first renewal.
  • Job to be done: when their broker tells them to go find business and hands them no system, they want an affordable on-ramp plus coaching, so they can get their first listings before they run out of runway.
  • The message that lands: “Start here. Prove it on one product. We will walk you through your first listing.” Anchor on Edward Estrada, 5 listings in 2 days, and Cindy Strait, 47 homes in her first year.
  • Where to reach them: new-agent communities, brokerage onboarding, social, and search for “how do I get listings as a new agent.”

3.3 Secondary Persona: The Hardcore Prospector

  • Who they are: the experienced agent who has always worked expireds and FSBOs by phone and lives by daily call discipline.
  • Job to be done: when they want to scale their volume without scaling their hours, they want the widest integrated channel system and the cleanest data, so they can reach more sellers per hour and add mail and ads to the phone.
  • The message that lands: “You already do the work. Run it through one system that does data, dialer, mail, and ads together.” Anchor on Paula Burlison ($522,000) and Kent Brown ($177K in 53 days).
  • Where to reach them: prospecting podcasts, role-play communities, and comparison searches against Vulcan7 and Mojo.

3.4 Secondary Persona: The Team Leader

  • Who they are: an agent running or building a team who needs the whole group prospecting the same way, on the same data, with shared visibility.
  • Job to be done: when they scale headcount, they want team functionality that brings the whole team to the same prospects, so they can hold a consistent prospecting standard across the team.
  • The message that lands: “Bring the whole team to the same prospects, on one system.” Anchor on Corinne Defilippis (up to 40 listings a month with REDX Teams) and Yisroel Weiss (solo to a 5-person team).
  • Where to reach them: team-leader and brokerage channels, recruiting conversations, and team-management content.

Figure 1. Cell shade indicates how strongly each pillar resonates with each persona. Use it to choose which pillar to lead with when opening a conversation with each audience - the work-willing prospector opens on coaching, the new agent on the price ladder, the hardcore prospector and team leader on the one-system pillar.

4. The Buyer’s Journey & Internal Voice

The buyer’s internal voice at each stage, the questions a work-willing agent is actually asking.

Awareness (the agent realizes they have a problem)

  • “I’m doing one or two deals a month and people around me are doing ten. Where is my next deal coming from?”
  • “My broker said go find business and handed me nothing. I don’t even know how to do that.”

Research (the agent is actively looking for solutions)

  • “Who do other prospecting agents actually use, and does it work for people like me?”
  • “Is this just a list of numbers, or is there something that shows me how to work them?”

Evaluation (the agent is comparing solutions)

  • “REDX, Vulcan7, Mojo, Landvoice. What is actually different, and which one won’t leave me alone with a phone?”
  • “I keep hearing the data has bad numbers. How bad, and what do they do about it?”

Purchase (the agent is considering the logistics of buying)

  • “Can I start small and prove it before I buy the whole thing?”
  • “What happens if it doesn’t work, and how hard is it to cancel?”

Use & Succeed (the agent is a user maximizing value)

  • “How do I fill my day with the activities that actually produce listings?”
  • “There is never enough time to get through all the leads. What do I work first?”

4.6 Trigger Events

The real-world moments that move an agent between stages. They power retargeting, re-engagement, and ICP scoring.

  • A new agent gets licensed and the broker says “go find business” with no system.
  • A pipeline runs dry and the agent realizes the next deal is unclear.
  • An agent watches a peer post a big month and asks what they are using.
  • A listing the agent wanted goes to a more active prospector down the street.
  • The agent reads about a TCPA settlement and worries the phone alone is a risk.
  • An agent’s current vendor delivers a batch of bad or DNC numbers and they start shopping.
  • A part-time agent decides whether to go full-time and needs proof the work pays off.

5. Core Messaging Strategy

5.1 Brand Promise

Do the work inside our system and our coaching, and you will list more homes. We hand you the complete prospecting system, the guide who walks alongside you, and the named agents who already proved it pays off.

5.2 Golden Formula, One-Liners & Taglines

Three finished options per slot, written fresh in the resolved voice. Never quote or characterize the company’s existing copy.

The Golden Formula: [target audience] who want to [goal] but struggle with [hurdle]. REDX helps them by [solution], so they can [outcome].

Golden Formula populated per persona:

  • The Work-Willing Prospector: Agents who want to list more homes but struggle to generate and work enough leads on their own. REDX gives them one system for data, dialer, mail, and ads plus a guide who walks them through it, so they can turn the work they are already willing to do into listings.
  • The New or Part-Time Agent: New agents who want their first listings but struggle because the broker handed them a phone and no plan. REDX gives them an affordable on-ramp and coaching, so they can get listing before they run out of runway.
  • The Hardcore Prospector: Veteran prospectors who want to scale volume but struggle to do it on the phone alone. REDX gives them the widest integrated channel system on cleaner data, so they can reach more sellers per hour across more channels.

Primary Tagline

  • three finished options: 1. “Do the work. We’ll show you the way.”
  • Rationale: puts the willing agent and the guide in one line, the core of the source of truth. 2. “The system that turns your work into listings.”
  • Rationale: names the outcome and the integrated whole in seven words. 3. “Every channel. One system. A guide who’s been there.”
  • Rationale: leads on integration plus the seasoned-guide identity. Recommended: Option 1, because it states the deal the brand makes, the agent supplies the work and REDX supplies the way, which is the durable promise.

Supporting Taglines - three per surface.

Website hero: 1. “Data, dialer, ads, mail, and content. One system, one guide.” 2. “Ask Kayla. Ask Len. Ask Cindy. They did the work here.” 3. “Not alone in the desert. Guided, the whole way to the listing.”

Signature / deck: 1. “REDX: the complete prospecting system, plus the coaching that makes it pay.” 2. “We train the prospectors. We teach them how to hunt.” 3. “Listings come from work. We make the work pay off.”

Value Hook

  • three finished options: 1. “Portal leads close under 1 percent. Agents who work REDX leads list homes by name.”
  • Rationale: pairs the defensible category stat with named proof. 2. “Five vendors, or one system that does it all and shows you how.”
  • Rationale: integration plus coaching against the bolt-it-together alternative. 3. “You bring the discipline. We bring the data, the channels, and the guide.”
  • Rationale: restates the work-willing deal with the integrated system named.

6. Product & Service Messaging

6.1 The REDX Platform

Built for the agent who is willing to prospect but tired of stitching together a data vendor, a dialer, a mailer, and an ad tool that never talk to each other. REDX is one integrated system: motivated-seller data feeds an outbound power dialer, and integrated ads, mail, and content keep sellers coming in while the agent works the phone. Team functionality brings the whole team to the same prospects. The outcome is a single workflow where the channels feed each other, so a willing agent spends their hours converting, not assembling tools.

6.2 Lead Products, Tools, and the Free Lead Manager

REDX’s published case studies confirm these products by name; positioning each below.

  • Expired Leads. Built for the prospector who wants sellers who already tried and failed to sell, the agents with a reason to move. Emily Peckham built 160+ FSBO listings over five years working this kind of lead with REDX, and Len Nevin makes $250,000 a year on expired leads.
  • FSBO Leads. Built for the agent who wants to reach for-sale-by-owner sellers before the competition. Steve Matthews, a struggling agent, generated $300,000 in commissions prospecting FSBOs.
  • Power Dialer. Built for the prospector who measures their day in conversations. Kent Brown made $177K in 53 days at 1,000 calls a day; Paula Burlison earns $522,000 listing homes with it.
  • Ad Builder. Built for the agent who wants sellers to come to them. Scott Catton reached $223K GCI using Ad Builder; Josh Sarangal prospects without cold calling.
  • Mail Merge / Mailers. Built for the agent who wants a steady touch in the mailbox alongside the phone. Cindy Strait, a new agent, listed 47 homes in one year using mail merge.
  • REDX Teams. Built for the team leader who needs the whole team prospecting the same way. Corinne Defilippis maintains up to 40 listings a month; Yisroel Weiss scaled solo to a 5-person team.
  • Vortex (free lead manager). Built for the agent who wants their leads organized and cross-checked automatically. Sam Gladstein reviews Vortex for more contacts per hour and automatic cross-checking against active listings.

[client to confirm before publication] any product tier names, GeoLeads coverage specifics, and any regulated certification or registration language before it appears in published copy.

6.3 Feature-Benefit Set

Map load-bearing features to the customer outcomes they deliver. Emotional-benefit cells are fresh samples in the customer’s voice register.

Feature Functional Benefit Emotional Benefit (sample customer-voice) Buying-Committee Role
Integrated data + dialer + ads + mail + content One workflow instead of five vendors “I can build my whole business with one tool.” Operator
Guided coaching (Bootcamp, University, live role-play) A daily roadmap for what to do “I’m not alone in the desert anymore. I know what to do today.” Operator / Manager
Named-agent proof library See an agent like you who already won “Someone exactly like me listed 47 homes their first year.” Operator / Exec
Outbound Power Dialer More conversations per hour “More conversation equals more conversion.” Operator
Confidence-rating filter on data Work the good numbers first “Less phone numbers at higher quality is exactly what I wanted.” Operator
Auto text and email follow-up Follow up without manual chasing “The auto text and email make it easier to follow up too.” Operator / Manager
Team functionality Whole team on the same prospects “I can bring the whole team to the same listings.” Manager / Exec
Affordable single-product entry Start small, prove it, scale up “The threshold to get started is just really low.” Finance / Operator
Vortex free lead manager Leads organized and cross-checked automatically “It saves me from manually searching, so I can serve my current clients.” Operator

7. Competitive Edge & Differentiators

7.1 Key Differentiators

  • The widest integrated channel system in the category. Data, dialer, ads, mail, content, and team functionality in one platform. Mojo centers on a dialer, Landvoice has no outbound dialer, Vulcan7 and Espresso are built around a single-line phone dialer with no native multi-channel orchestration.
  • The deepest named-outcome proof library. A full wall of named agents with real numbers, more named proof than any rival publishes. This is the rarest asset in the category.
  • Guided coaching paired with the system. Bootcamp, University, live role-play, and community, framed as the guide who walks alongside the agent. Rivals coach, but none pair coaching with the widest channel system and the deepest proof.
  • A price ladder that wins the entrant and the ROI buyer. A low single-product entry that climbs to a premium full system, against rivals who price the entry and the bundle as separate stories.
  • Parity-priced data against a reputation-only premium. Once hygiene is fixed, best-in-class data at parity with Vulcan7’s “Bentley” premium, at lower cost, where the premium rests on branding and inconsistent figures.

7.2 Objection-Handling Library

Each firm response leads with the claim and ends in an operative verb. Each proof anchor is a named agent, a category stat, or a structural argument; any factual anchor is ledger-sourced or tagged for confirmation.

Objection (buyer voice, sample) Firm Response (resolved voice, sample) Proof Anchor
“I only have a few listings a year, I’m too small for a whole platform.” “Start on one product at the entry price, prove it, and scale up only when it pays. The ladder is built for exactly your size, so start small.” Price ladder; entry at the low end of the category
“I’m seasonal, I don’t prospect year-round, why pay every month?” “Run the system in your selling season and lean on mail and ads to keep sellers warm in the off months, so you don’t restart cold every spring.” Integrated mail/ads + content channels
“I got billed after I tried to cancel.” “Cancellation should be clean, and we set the terms up front so there’s no surprise renewal. Ask exactly how cancellation works before you buy, and hold us to it.” [client to confirm cancellation policy and terms before publication]
“Your own reviews say the data is full of bad and DNC numbers.” “We’re fixing data hygiene first and setting honest accuracy expectations at onboarding, and the confidence-rating filter puts the good numbers in front of you. Judge us on the fixed experience, then weigh the price.” Data-hygiene fix (Pillar 5); confidence-rating filter
“Vulcan7 has better data, everyone says so.” “Their premium is a reputation, not a measured edge, and their own pages can’t agree whether it’s 14 percent or 20 percent more numbers. Compare the data at parity and keep the lower price.” Vulcan7 inconsistent “14% vs 20%” figures; reputation-only premium
“Motivated seller leads, every vendor says the same thing.” “Skip the label and look at the agents. Emily Peckham closed 160+ FSBOs in five years here; portal leads close under 1 percent. Compare the named outcomes.” Emily Peckham; 0.4-1.2% portal conversion
“I buy my leads from a reseller, who supports me when something breaks?” “Talk to us about how support and the coaching curriculum reach you through your reseller, and confirm who owns the relationship before you commit.” [client to confirm reseller/partner support model before publication]
“Isn’t REDX just owned by a bigger company that doesn’t care about agents?” “Judge the company by what its agents say, not by a corporate label. Read the named case studies and talk to agents using it, then decide.” Named-agent proof library
“It’s just a list of numbers, I can get data anywhere.” “Buy the system, not a list. Data feeds the dialer, the dialer feeds follow-up, and mail and ads bring sellers in, with a guide showing you how. Compare the workflow, not the list.” Integrated system (Pillar 1)
“Coaching is just videos of lucky agents showing off.” “Our coaching is live role-play and a working community, and the proof is agents like you who listed homes. Sit in on a role-play session before you judge it.” Live role-play; named new-agent proof (Estrada, Strait)
“I tried cold calling, the regulations make it too risky now.” “Manual expired and FSBO calls are still legal, and the real risk is litigation, which is exactly why you run mail and ads alongside the phone. Diversify your channels and lower the risk.” TCPA litigation surge; manual-call carve-out
“It’s too expensive once I add everything up.” “Each piece pays for itself when you work it, and agents show the return by name. Start at the on-ramp price and add channels as they earn their place.” Price ladder; named-agent ROI (Burlison, Catton)

8. Brand Voice & Personality

A voice a copywriter can write in, not a brand book.

8.1 Brand Essence

The seasoned guide who hands a willing agent the complete system and walks alongside them until the work becomes listings.

8.2 Voice Archetype

The primary voice is the Experienced Guide: a frontier scout who has hunted this ground for years and now trains the newcomers, equal parts Tony Robbins’ disciplined encouragement and the calm certainty of a mentor who has seen the path. Leadership named this directly across the interviews: the “experienced frontiersman” who trains the cowboys and teaches them to hunt, the mentor “walking alongside” the agent so they are never “alone in the desert,” and the Tony Robbins register that “shows there’s a path to success” through discipline and compounding consistency. The voice is warm, direct, and motivating without hype. It respects the agent’s willingness to work and points them to the path.

We Say (resolved-voice sample) We Do Not Say (the rejected generic register)
“Do the work. We’ll show you the way.” “Industry-leading lead generation solutions.”
“Ask Kayla. She listed her way to $14M.” “Our customers love our results.”
“You’re not alone in the desert. We walk it with you.” “Best-in-class onboarding experience.”
“Portal leads close under 1 percent. Work a seller who has to move.” “High-quality motivated seller leads.”
“Start small. Prove it. Then scale.” “Affordable pricing for every budget.”
“One system. Every channel. A guide who’s been there.” “All-in-one platform with cutting-edge tools.”
“We’re fixing the data, and we’ll tell you the truth about it.” “The most accurate data in the industry.”
“Your discipline plus our system equals listings.” “Unlock your full potential with REDX.”

Tone calibration

  • Marketing / website / outbound: motivating and plainspoken, the guide pointing at a real path and the agents who walked it. Concrete names and numbers, never hype.
  • Sales / 1:1: the mentor across the table. Diagnose the agent’s real pain in their words first, then show the path. Calm certainty, no pressure.
  • Support / post-purchase: the steady hand. Honest about what to expect, especially on data, and quick to point to the next right action.

8.3 Personality

  • Archetype: the Sage as primary (the experienced guide who knows the path), with a Hero supporting note for the agent’s own journey. The brand is the guide; the agent is the one who acts.
  • Personality traits: seasoned, disciplined, encouraging, plainspoken, honest.
  • How they make others feel: like Neo seeing the pattern, certain, in control, and clear on exactly what to do next.

9. Channel Application Examples

Fresh sample copy in the resolved voice for the surfaces teams ship most.

9.1 Sample Homepage Hero (three finished options)

  • H1: “The complete prospecting system, and the coaching that turns your work into listings.” Sub-H1: “Data, dialer, ads, mail, and content in one system, with a guide who walks you through it. Ask Kayla, Len, and Cindy.” CTA: “Start prospecting.” Rationale: leads on the integrated whole plus the guide and names the proof.
  • H1: “Do the work. We’ll show you the way.” Sub-H1: “One system for every prospecting channel, plus the coaching and the named agents who prove it pays off.” CTA: “See the system.” Rationale: states the brand’s core deal in five words, then explains it.
  • H1: “You’re willing to do the work. You just need the way.” Sub-H1: “REDX gives willing agents the complete system and a guide who’s been there, so the effort you already spend turns into listings.” CTA: “Get the roadmap.” Rationale: speaks straight to the work-willing agent’s self-image. Recommended: Option 1, because it carries the integrated system, the coaching, and named proof in one scannable hero, the whole source of truth above the fold.

9.2 Sample LinkedIn Ad Headlines (three per persona)

The Work-Willing Prospector: 1. “You’ll do the work. We’ll show you the way to listings.” 2. “One or two deals a month? See how Kayla hit $14M.” 3. “More conversations, more listings. One system does it all.”

The New or Part-Time Agent: 1. “New agent, no roadmap? Start here and list your first home.” 2. “Cindy listed 47 homes her first year. Here’s how.” 3. “Your broker said go find business. We’ll show you how.”

The Hardcore Prospector: 1. “Stop paying five vendors. Run every channel in one system.” 2. “1,000 calls a day got Kent $177K in 53 days.” 3. “Scale your prospecting without scaling your hours.”

The Team Leader: 1. “Bring your whole team to the same prospects, one system.” 2. “Corinne maintains 40 listings a month with REDX Teams.” 3. “One prospecting standard, your whole team, one platform.”

9.3 Sample Cold-Email Openers (two per trigger event)

Trigger: new agent, broker said “go find business” with no system. 1. “Your broker told you to go find business and handed you a phone. We hand new agents a system and a guide. Cindy Strait listed 47 homes her first year doing exactly that. Want to see how she started?” 2. “Most new agents quit before their first renewal because nobody showed them how to prospect. We do. Here’s the on-ramp that gets you your first listings.”

Trigger: pipeline ran dry, next deal unclear. 1. “When the pipeline’s empty, the question is always the same: where’s the next deal coming from? Here’s the system that answers it every morning.” 2. “A slow month is a system problem, not a you problem. Here’s the daily prospecting roadmap that keeps the pipeline full.”

Trigger: watched a peer post a big month. 1. “You saw what they posted this month. They’re not working harder than you, they’re working a system. Here’s the one, with the agents who prove it.” 2. “The agent down the street isn’t lucky, they’re running every channel from one system. Want the same setup?”

Trigger: a wanted listing went to a more active prospector. 1. “That listing went to whoever reached the seller first. Here’s how to be that agent, with data, a dialer, and mail working together.” 2. “You lost that one on speed and reach. One system gives you both. Here’s how it works.”

Trigger: read about a TCPA settlement, worried the phone is a risk. 1. “The TCPA settlements are real, and the smart move isn’t dropping the phone, it’s adding channels around it. Here’s how mail and ads keep your pipeline full while you dial less.” 2. “Litigation risk is rising and manual expired calls are still legal. The hedge is a multi-channel system. Here’s the one built for it.”

Trigger: current vendor delivered bad or DNC numbers. 1. “Tired of a batch full of disconnected and DNC numbers? We’re fixing data hygiene and we’ll tell you the truth about it, with a filter that puts the good numbers first. Want to compare?” 2. “Bad numbers waste your best hours. Here’s how our confidence-rating filter and honest onboarding change that. Worth a look?”

Trigger: part-time agent deciding whether to go full-time. 1. “Going full-time is a leap. Len Nevin made it on expired leads and now earns $250,000. Here’s the system and coaching he used.” 2. “Before you commit full-time, see the proof the work pays off. Here’s the on-ramp built for part-timers ready to scale.”

9.4 Sample Sales-Deck Cover Slide

Title: “Do the work. We’ll show you the way.” Subtitle: “The complete prospecting system, plus the coaching that turns a willing agent’s work into listings.” Recommended visual anchor: a single trail through open country with a seasoned guide a step ahead of an agent, suggesting the frontier-guide identity and the path, not a screenshot of tools.

9.5 Sales Talk-Tracks (one per persona)

The Work-Willing Prospector. Diagnostic opener: “How many real conversations with sellers are you having in a normal week right now?” Reframe: “More conversation is more conversion, and the gap between you and the agents doing ten deals a month is almost always a system, not effort. You’re already willing to work; you just need the way.” The move: walk them through the integrated system and the named agent who looks most like them, then start them at the rung that fits.

The New or Part-Time Agent. Diagnostic opener: “When your broker said go find business, what did they actually hand you to do it with?” Reframe: “Most new agents wash out because nobody gave them a roadmap, not because they weren’t willing. We give you the on-ramp and a guide so you list your first homes before the runway runs out.” The move: show Cindy Strait and Edward Estrada, then start them on the entry product with the coaching path.

The Hardcore Prospector. Diagnostic opener: “How many separate tools are you paying for to run your prospecting right now?” Reframe: “You already have the discipline. The leverage is running data, dialer, mail, and ads from one system instead of stitching five together, so you reach more sellers per hour across more channels.” The move: show the integrated workflow and the per-hour gain, then move them to the full system.

The Team Leader. Diagnostic opener: “How do you keep your whole team prospecting the same way on the same data?” Reframe: “A team is only as strong as its weakest prospecting habit. Team functionality puts everyone on the same prospects with shared visibility, so the standard holds across the team.” The move: show Corinne Defilippis and Yisroel Weiss, then scope REDX Teams to the team’s size.

9.6 Customer-Success / Lifecycle Email Templates

Three editable templates as bracketed-variable sample copy in the resolved voice.

Onboarding. Subject: “[First name], here’s your first week.” “Welcome to REDX, [First name]. You’re here because you’re willing to do the work, and our job is to show you the way. This week, start with [product], make your first [number] dials, and join the live role-play on [day]. One honest note up front: the data pulls from many sources, so use the confidence-rating filter to work the strongest numbers first. Your guide for week one is [coach/resource]. Reply to this email any time you’re stuck.”

Mid-term check-in. Subject: “[First name], how’s the prospecting going?” “You’re [time] into REDX, [First name]. The agents who get the most out of this are the ones who lean on the coaching, so a quick check: are you getting through your leads, and are you in the role-play sessions? If the leads are piling up, that’s normal, let’s prioritize what to work first. Here’s the next training that fits where you are: [resource]. Want a 15-minute call with [coach] to tune your daily plan?”

  • Renewal (framed as continuation, not a re-sell). Subject: “[First name], let’s keep the momentum going.” “You’ve been prospecting with REDX for [time], [First name], and the work is starting to compound. Renewing keeps your data, your dialer, your coaching, and your community right where they are, with nothing to rebuild. If you’re ready to add a channel, [next product] is the common next step for agents at your stage. Want to talk through what’s working before we continue?”

10. Banned-Word Glossary

In addition to the universal AI-tells bans. Do not use any listed phrase elsewhere in the Guide.

Banned / Avoid Use Instead Why
“All-in-one platform” “One system for every prospecting channel” Table-stakes phrase used by Vulcan7, Espresso, and Mojo; says nothing differentiating.
“Motivated seller leads” “Sellers who have a reason to move” or a named-agent outcome Every vendor in the category uses the exact phrase; it differentiates nothing.
“Industry-leading data” “Data at parity with the premium competitor, at lower cost” Unprovable, table-stakes, and contradicted by REDX’s own reviews until hygiene is fixed.
“Best-in-class data” (as a headline) “Honest data you can hold us to” until the fix clears Customers report bad numbers; lead on the fix, then claim parity.
“Most accurate data” “We’re fixing data hygiene and setting honest expectations” A purity claim the lived customer experience contradicts.
“44% relist / 29% list / 3x homes / 40-50% conversion” Named-agent outcomes and the 0.4-1.2% portal contrast Unaudited internal figures; [client to confirm], never headline.
“Cutting-edge tools” Name the tool and what it does Decoration that signals thin substance to a sharp agent.
“Unlock your potential” “Turn your work into listings” Hollow motivational filler; name the concrete outcome.
“The rules are tightening” “Litigation risk is rising, so diversify channels” The FCC consent rule was vacated; the accurate story is litigation risk.
“Passive income / leads while you sleep” “Do the work, and the system makes it pay” Attracts the buyer who churns; the brand targets the work-willing agent.
“Game-changer” State the specific change and name the agent who got it Empty intensifier; proof carries the weight.
“Seamless integration” “Data feeds the dialer, the dialer feeds follow-up” Describe the actual workflow, not a buzzword.
“Revolutionary / next-generation” Name the feature and the outcome Grand language reads as a tell that the substance is thin.

11. What Success Looks Like

Qualitative, directional outcomes if the positioning lands.

First 90 Days

  • The surface tells the truth: the homepage and navigation lead with the system-plus-coaching identity and named-agent proof, education has moved out of the support navigation, and the banned figures are gone from public copy. Leading indicator: count the public surfaces still carrying a banned claim, and the share of pages leading with named proof.

First Year

  • The experience earns the promise: the data-hygiene fix has shipped, the accuracy complaint is receding, and the parity-at-lower-cost line is live behind a measured benchmark. The new-agent on-ramp is packaged with coaching and named new-agent proof. Leading indicator: count accuracy-themed complaints across review sites month over month, and the share of new customers entering through the on-ramp.

Steady State

  • The proof compounds: new agents enter on the affordable on-ramp, the coaching converts their effort into listings, and each new named outcome feeds the proof library that sells the next agent. The market reaches for REDX when an agent decides to get serious about prospecting. Leading indicator: count net new named case studies added per period, and the rate of inbound prospects who cite a named agent.

12. Final Takeaways

  • Takeaway 1: REDX is the complete prospecting system plus the coaching that turns a willing agent’s work into listings.
  • Takeaway 2: The edge is the widest integrated channel system, the deepest named-agent proof, and a guide who walks alongside the agent.
  • Takeaway 3: The agent does the work; REDX hands them the system, the path, and the proof it pays off.
  • Takeaway 4: The messaging centers the work-willing agent, from the new agent finding their first listing to the team leader scaling one.
  • Takeaway 5: REDX sells a system you work, so it leaves the passive-lead buyer to someone else.

Appendix A. Rationale for the Affordable-On-Ramp-into-Premium Price Position

Recipients are most likely to challenge the price ladder with “are we cheap or premium,” so the defense sits here.

  • The category prices the entry and the bundle as separate stories: Landvoice runs $87 to $197 a month with a $40 FSBO entry, Vulcan7’s full bundle runs about $359 a month and is called the most expensive in its class, and Espresso’s Pro plan is $279 a month.
  • REDX already sits at the low end on single-product entry and climbs to a premium full system, so the ladder describes what the pricing already is.
  • The underserved new and part-time agent is priced out of the $2,000-plus-a-month team tech stack, and over 80 percent of new agents leave before their first renewal for lack of prospecting support, so the low on-ramp reaches a real, evidenced segment.
  • The ROI rung sells on named proof: agents show the return by name, which lets the full system carry a premium price as proven ROI rather than an expense.
  • The risk to manage is that the cheap entry anchors price perception; the mitigation is to message the entry as an on-ramp with a clear upgrade path, confirmed by first-party data on where buyers actually balk.
